narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 52.8% chance that you would not pass—crossing the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction stemmed primarily from your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which stood out as the strongest factor pushing the model toward forecasting a bar failure, since it placed you well below the typical top-third standing of students who succeed. In contrast, your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 acted as a helpful counterbalance, pulling the prediction toward success because it was close to the 3.24 average among those who passed, showing solid prior performance. Your Year 1 decile of 3 also influenced the outcome, though less powerfully than Year 3, by nudging the model slightly toward expecting a failure compared to the higher sixth-decile average for passers. While your middle family income aligns with over a third of successful students and your full-time enrollment matches nearly all of them, and your LSAT score of 31.5 was a consideration, these did not outweigh the key academic decile concerns. Overall, your profile compared to admitted students—who typically rank in the top half or better in school deciles and score around 37 on the LSAT—highlighted gaps in later-year performance that the model weighed heavily. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven signal based on patterns from past students. To strengthen a future application, focus on boosting your academic deciles through targeted study habits or coursework, perhaps retaking the LSAT to aim closer to 37 or above. Consider gaining practical experience, like internships, that could indirectly support stronger law school performance. You might also explore programs with bar passage support tailored for full-time students from middle-income families. Reviewing your transcript for trends between Year 1 and Year 3 could reveal specific areas to address. With targeted improvements, your solid UGPA foundation positions you well to shift this prediction in your favor next time. Keep pushing forward—many students turn these insights into success stories.
